petitioner_background: The petitioner is a ballroom dancer and instructor who participates in competitions and events in the United States and Europe and instructs students in her local area. She sought classification as an individual of extraordinary ability in the arts under INA § 203(b)(1)(A) (EB-1A). The record reflects she previously received O-1 nonimmigrant status. She has competed at national and international dancesport championships and taught students who have won dancesport competitions.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: First place finish at the 2015 [competition], first place finish at the 2009 [competition], and second place finish at the 2008 [competition]. Additionally documented a second place finish at the 2012 [competition], a third place finish at the 2012 [competition], and a first place finish at the 2009 [competition].
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director found the petitioner met the awards criterion based on her first, first, and second place finishes. On final merits review, the AAO acknowledged the petitioner has received nationally or internationally recognized dancesport awards, but determined she did not demonstrate that such achievements have garnered her sustained national or international acclaim and a 'career of acclaimed work in the field' as contemplated by Congress (H.R. Rep. No. 101-723, 59 (Sept. 19, 1990)).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Documentation of the petitioner's membership with two organizations (names redacted).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence did not demonstrate that outstanding achievements are required for membership. For one organization, individuals are able to register rather than being granted membership based on outstanding achievements judged by recognized national or international experts. For the other, the registration requirements provide minimum standards such as residency, education (adjudicators), and competition experience (national judges), but these are not indicative of expertly evaluated outstanding achievements. Additional evidence reflected full and probationary membership requirements for organizations wishing to join (not individuals). The petitioner did not establish that the membership requirements are tantamount to outstanding achievements.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Various screenshots and articles listing the petitioner's finishes alongside placements of other dancers at competitions; photographs of the petitioner with captions reflecting advertisements for shows; blurbs for dance shows in local publications; and a 2012 article from a publication (name redacted) that was indicative of published material about the petitioner relating to her work.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found most materials merely mention the petitioner as a competitor but are not published material about her — simply listing her finishes alongside other dancers. The evidence mostly contained photographs with captions reflecting advertisements rather than published material discussing her work. Blurbs in local publications did not qualify as articles in professional or major trade publications or other major media. While the 2012 article was indicative of published material about her, the petitioner did not establish that the publication is a professional or major trade publication or other major medium. Furthermore, a single article published approximately three years prior to the filing of the petition is not consistent with sustained national or international acclaim required for this highly restrictive classification.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner judged dancers from studios in the northeastern region of the United States at an event in Connecticut in 2015, and served as a judge at an event in New York in 2014.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director found the petitioner met this criterion based on her participation as a judge. On final merits review, the AAO evaluated the significance of her judging experience under Kazarian and found that without evidence that sets the petitioner apart from others in her field — such as evidence that she has served as a judge of acclaimed ballroom dancers or of a prestigious national or international competition rather than students or amateurs at local dance studios — the record does not demonstrate that she is one of the small percentage who has risen to the very top of the field of endeavor.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Education credentials from two institutions; a letter from a national examiner stating the petitioner 'is most highly trained in all four styles of ballroom dancing' and that the examiner is personally training the petitioner to hopefully become an examiner, and that '[s]he would be a great asset to our industry'; evidence that she has taught three students who have won various dancesport competitions, including the 2015 [competition].
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not explain how she has used her training to influence the ballroom dancing or dancesport field in a significant way. Having a diverse skill set is not a contribution of major significance in and of itself. The record must be supported by evidence that the petitioner has already used those unique skills to impact the field. The examiner's letter described future applicability and determinations that may occur at a later date, so the actual impact on the field had yet to be determined. Regarding her students' wins, the AAO found the petitioner did not demonstrate how those awards are original contributions of major significance to the field; rather, the record indicates that the petitioner's teaching and impact were limited to her students.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner indicated she wrote two treatises regarding the teaching of dancesport that were published by universities in Russia. She presented an uncertified English language translation for the first page of one document, and submitted an uncertified translation of a cover page for another, without providing a copy and translation of the entire documents.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that because the petitioner did not provide full and certified translations as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), the submitted articles have diminished probative value. The petitioner did not show that her papers were peer-reviewed, contained any references to sources, or were otherwise considered 'scholarly,' and did not demonstrate her work was published in professional or major trade publications or other major media. The record did not contain documentary evidence reflecting the citation of her papers. Citation history or other evidence of the impact of the petitioner's articles is often evaluated when determining significance to the field. The petitioner did not demonstrate that her papers have attracted a level of interest in her field commensurate with sustained national or international acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner performed as part of a dancing tour at a venue in Connecticut, and performed at local exhibitions including venues in Connecticut and New York.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director/AAO found the petitioner satisfied the display criterion as initial evidence. On final merits review, the AAO noted that as it is expected that a dancer would perform in a dance setting at festivals, exhibitions, and other events, it evaluated the extent to which the display of her work reflected acclaim. The petitioner did not establish that the venues are considered prestigious or popular venues, or that her exhibitions garnered attention in a manner consistent with sustained national or international acclaim. The petitioner did not demonstrate that her performances brought praise from critics, drew record crowds, or raised attendance. Without evidence distinguishing the petitioner's shows from others in her field, she has not shown she is one of that small percentage who has risen to the very top of the field of endeavor.

outcome_reasoning: Upon de novo review, the AAO dismissed the appeal. Although the petitioner met three regulatory criteria (awards, judging, and exhibition/display), the AAO conducted a final merits determination and concluded that her achievements in the aggregate confirmed she is a talented dancer who has performed in front of audiences, received awards, and judged other dancers, but do not demonstrate she has sustained national or international acclaim or that she is one of the small percentage at the very top of her field of endeavor. The AAO also noted that the prior approval of an O-1 nonimmigrant petition does not preclude USCIS from denying an immigrant visa petition adjudicated based on a different standard. The petitioner did not meet her burden to establish eligibility for the classification under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act.
legal_citations: Section 203(b) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011), aff'd, 683 F.3d 1030 (9th Cir. 2012)
QData Consulting, Inc. v. INS, 293 F. Supp. 2d 25 (D.D.C. 2003)
IKEA US v. US Dept. of Justice, 48 F. Supp. 2d 22 (D.D.C. 1999)
Fedin Brothers Co. Ltd., 724 F. Supp. at 1103
Louisiana Philharmonic Orchestra v. INS, No. 98-2855, 2000 WL 282785 (E.D. La.), aff'd, 248 F.3d 1139 (5th Cir. 2001), cert. denied, 122 S.Ct. 51 (2001)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The petitioner submitted uncertified English language translations of foreign language documents (Russian treatises), in violation of 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), which requires foreign language documents to be accompanied by a full English language translation certified as complete and accurate by a competent translator. As a result, those submitted articles were given diminished probative value. Additionally, the petitioner indicated she won additional dancing awards but did not document the record of proceedings.
